titleStmt.title: Letter from Thomas Gill to John Redmond, 11 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Thomas Patrick Gill

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from Thomas Gill (1858-1931) to John Redmond (1856-1918) requesting a special grant for food production supplies.
In 1916 Thomas Patrick Gill was serving as the secretary for the Department of Agriculture. He also worked with the Department of Technical Instruction from its establishment in 1900 until 1923.
John Redmond (1856-1918) was an MP and leader of the Irish Parliamentary Party. He was instrumental in securing the promise of Home Rule from Asquith's liberal government. Upon the outbreak of war, Redmond addressed a group of Irish Volunteers at Woodenbridge, Co. Wicklow, encouraging them to fight with the British Army to ensure the implementation of Home Rule. Redmond's words split the Irish Volunteers across the country. The Easter Rising shocked Redmond, who condemned it bitterly. Nonetheless he appealed for leniency towards those who had not been involved in planning the Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: John Redmond Papers, Collection List No. 118; MS 15,193/3/5

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Private DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE
AND TECHNICAL INSTRUCTION.
DUBLIN. 11th April, 1916. My dear Redmond, Special grant for food-production
schemes, &c. I enclose you copy of a note I had to-day from
Runciman promising to support our application for this
in the Cabinet. (This time last year when I saw him, as
Chairman of the National Food-Supply Committee of the
Cabinet, he promised me to do the same, but the
application was then deferred). I sent you con-
fidentially particulars of the application and of the
case for it. I think you ought to see Montagu and
McKenna (I do not expect much out of the former without
the backing of the latter) and let them know Runciman's
view. Runciman is the member of the Cabinet who knows
most about the food-supply problem. Birrell is probably
the man who should bring it forward in the Cabinet, and
I have written him and also Russell a line telling them
of Runciman's support; but it is most important to
emphasise that this application is, and should be pressed
as, an Imperial question, not as an Irish question. Also,
in J.E. Redmond, Esq., M.P.
in view of all the precariousness attending the food-
supply problem, it is a question of some urgency. Yours ever, T.P. Gill l
COPY. BOARD OF TRADE,
WHITEHALL GARDENS, S.W. 10th April, 1916. Dear Sir, In reply to your letter of the 5th April,
Mr.Runciman has asked me to say that he will support
your proposal for a special grant for your
Department to save your food production schemes from
the effects of the shrinkage of your funds due to the
War. Yours very truly, (sgd.) R.W.Matthews. T. P. Gill, Esq.
